old_claim.note: Classical Sculpture provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Romanesque sculpture revived monumental sculpture after a long post-Roman dormancy and positioned itself against the classical figural tradition, making classical sculpture historical lineage rather than an enabling technique.

source_shape.source_locator: Romanesque art sculpture section: Britannica says monumental sculpture was revived in western Europe after almost 600 years of dormancy and describes stylistic freedom from the classical figural tradition.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places Romanesque sculpture after a long post-Roman gap and frames classical sculpture as an earlier tradition rather than a direct technique requirement.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a historical_predecessor edge because the relationship is chronological and stylistic lineage; Romanesque/Gothic art did not require classical sculpture as an operational prerequisite.

ontology_before: Fine Arts (Medieval) treated Classical Sculpture as a generic enabling prerequisite, implying a direct technique dependency.
ontology_after: Romanesque and Gothic Fine Arts retain Classical Sculpture only as historical and stylistic predecessor context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: fine_arts_medieval -> classical_sculpture is now historical_predecessor.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to all medieval art traditions rather than Romanesque and Gothic fine arts.
A source showed Romanesque or Gothic workshops required direct continuity of classical sculptural technique.
The edge were replaced by a more specific Roman imperial sculpture or early Christian sculpture predecessor node.
short_reason: Classical sculpture is lineage context for Romanesque/Gothic art, not an enabling method.
